sections.current_situation:
The annual budget for public art projects is approved by the Civic Arts Commission
every fiscal year. The approved Public Art Budget for FY26 pertains to funding for public
art projects located in two distinct funds: Fund 148 (Cultural Trust Fund); and Fund 150
(Public Art Fund):
Fund 148: Cultural Trust Fund Budget for FY26 (Attachment 1) includes a total of
$2,860,919 to be used for public art. The City’s Percentage for Public Art on Private
Projects Ordinance (Berkeley Municipal Code Chapter 23.316, established in 2016)
generates in-lieu fees from private development to the Cultural Trust Fund (Fund 148)
valued at 0.8% of a private development’s construction costs for any of the following:
new multifamily residential projects of five or more dwelling units; new commercial
structures; new industrial structures; and all construction of building additions exceeding
10,000 square feet. This budget includes committed and uncommitted rollover of the

The Cultural Trust Fund Budget contains projected expenditures totaling $2,860,919
including, but not limited to:
 Commissioning artwork for Santa Fe Railroad Right of Way;
 Pilot Utility Box Mural Program;
 Completion of Mosaic artwork for Aquatic Park; and
 Artwork exhibitions in the City’s “Cube Space” gallery in the Center Street
Garage.
The budget includes artwork conservation contracts to maintain and repair public art
owned by the City; administration funds set-aside; and a contingency amount for use
during the implementation of the FY26 projects. The FY26 budget also includes funds
for a Public Art Stra
sections.background:
The City’s public art funding policies require the Civic Arts Commission to develop an
annual budget for public art projects. The Public Art on Private Development policy also
requires a regular annual public process for deciding how the Cultural Trust Funds will
be allocated in accordance with the priorities of the Arts and Cultural Plan, following the
Guidelines for Public Art in the City of Berkeley. Each year, Civic Arts staff works with
the Civic Arts Commission’s Public Art Subcommittee and other City departments to
develop these budgets simultaneously.
Last fiscal year (FY25), the Civic Arts Program implemented a portfolio of public art
projects valued at $696,808 with funds allocated in the FY25 Public Art Program
budgets. Those projects included:
 Completed the commissioning and installation of 76 framed works by
photographer Lara Kaur and organized an unveiling event at the West Berkeley
Family Wellness Center Open House (June 27, 2025).
 Finalized the design and construction approval of a mosaic artwork by Julie W.
Chang and coordinated artwork proposal display as part of the Willard Park
Clubhouse ribbon cutting and open house (June 25, 2025).
 Completed the purchase and installation of 24 framed works by Bay Area artists
curated by Kala Art Institute and installed new artwork signage at 2180 Milvia
Street and 1947 Center Street (June 16, 2025).
 Issued a nationwide RFQ and selected artist Sorell Raino Tsui for the Martin
Luther King Jr. Youth Services Center/Young Adult Project public art commission
at Grove Park.
 Developed an approved Project Plan for two free-standing public art structures
as part of the Santa Fe Trackbed-to-Park conversion.
 Executed a contract with artist Masako Miki for a public art installation at the
Aquatic Park pathway wall.
 Presented four quarterly exhibitions featuring emerging and mid-career Bay Area
artists at the Cube Space gallery (2010 Addison Street).
 Issued a nationwide RFP for a Public Art Program Strategic

sections.preamble:
Page 1 of 6
38
Civic Arts Commission
INFORMATION CALENDAR
October 28, 2025
To: Honorable Mayor and Members of the City Council
From: Civic Arts Commission
Submitted by: Cameron Woo, Chairperson, Civic Arts Commission
Subject: Fiscal Year 2026 Public Art Budget
INTRODUCTION
Attached is the City of Berkeley’s Public Art Budget for Fiscal Year 2026 (FY26). The
Civic Arts Commission-approved FY26 Public Art Budget will guide the City’s public art
investments and project management for the upcoming fiscal year, implemented by the
Office of Economic Development’s Civic Arts Program, in collaboration with other City
departments, consultant teams, commissioned artists, project stakeholders, and
communities.
The FY26 Public Art Budget was adopted by the Civic Arts Commission at its regularly
scheduled meeting on June 25, 2025, as follows: M/S/C (Blecher/Woo) to approve
FY26 Public Art Budget. Vote: Ayes — Bachrach, Blecher, Bullwinkel, Montgomery,
Woo; Nays — None; Abstain — None; Absent — Pineda, Scott. In this fiscal year, the
Civic Arts Commission has prioritized ensuring adequate funding for current public art
projects underway, completion of the current multiyear and not yet started public art
projects identified in previous fiscal years, and conducting a Public Art Strategic Plan
before initiating new public art projects.
